items.title: Conversation about a history presentation
items.ttsText: Narrator. Listen to a conversation between a student and a professor. Student. Professor Martin, do you have a moment? I wanted to ask about my history presentation. Professor. Of course. Is this about your topic on early trade routes? Student. Yes. I have collected a lot of information, but I am worried that my presentation is too broad. Professor. That is a common problem. Which part of the topic interests you most? Student. I am most interested in how trade routes helped spread ideas, not just goods. Professor. That is a stronger focus. You could organize your presentation around three examples: religious ideas, scientific knowledge, and artistic styles. Student. That sounds clearer. Should I still include a map? Professor. Definitely. A map will help your classmates understand how the regions were connected. Student. Great. I will revise my outline tonight. Narrator. Now answer the questions.

questions.question: Why does the student visit the professor?
choices: To ask for advice about a presentation
To request a new history textbook
questions.explanation: The student asks the professor for help with his history presentation.

questions.question: What is the student's original concern?
choices: His topic may be too broad
He has not chosen a topic
questions.explanation: The student says he is worried that his presentation is too broad.

questions.question: What focus does the professor suggest?
questions.explanation: The professor agrees that focusing on how trade routes spread ideas is stronger.

questions.question: What examples does the professor mention?
choices: Religious ideas, scientific knowledge, and artistic styles
questions.explanation: The professor suggests organizing the presentation around those three examples.

questions.question: Why should the student include a map?
choices: To show how regions were connected
To show the professor's office location
questions.explanation: The professor says a map will help classmates understand the connections between regions.

items.title: Conversation about a campus job
items.ttsText: Narrator. Listen to a conversation between a student and an employee at the campus employment office. Student. Hello. I saw an announcement about student assistant positions in the computer lab. Are those jobs still available? Employee. Yes, but only two positions are left. Have you worked in a computer lab before? Student. Not officially, but I helped students use software in my high school media center. Employee. That experience may be useful. The main duties here are checking students in, helping with printing problems, and reporting equipment issues. Student. What hours are available? Employee. We need someone on Monday and Wednesday evenings from six to nine. Student. That works with my class schedule. Do I need to submit a resume? Employee. Yes. Submit a resume and a short application form by Friday afternoon. Interviews will be held next week. Narrator. Now answer the questions.

questions.question: What is the student interested in?
choices: A student assistant job in the computer lab
questions.explanation: The student asks about student assistant positions in the computer lab.

questions.question: What experience does the student mention?
choices: Helping students use software in a media center
questions.explanation: The student helped students use software in his high school media center.

questions.question: Which duty is mentioned for the job?
questions.explanation: The employee says the job includes helping students with printing problems.

questions.question: When are the available work hours?
questions.explanation: The employee says the lab needs someone Monday and Wednesday evenings from six to nine.

questions.question: What must the student submit by Friday?
questions.explanation: The employee tells the student to submit a resume and a short application form by Friday afternoon.

items.title: Conversation about a missing notebook
items.ttsText: Narrator. Listen to a conversation between two students. Woman. Hey, Daniel, did you see a blue notebook in the study room yesterday? Man. A blue notebook? I remember seeing one on the table near the window. Was it yours? Woman. Yes. It has all my chemistry notes in it, and I have a quiz tomorrow. Man. I left the study room around five, and the notebook was still there. Did you check the lost and found desk? Woman. Not yet. I was hoping someone from our study group picked it up. Man. I can text the group, but you should also check the library desk. Sometimes the staff collects items at the end of the day. Woman. Good idea. I will go there now. Man. If you cannot find it, I can lend you my notes from last week. Narrator. Now answer the questions.

questions.question: What is the woman's problem?
choices: She forgot the study group time
questions.explanation: The woman says her blue notebook with chemistry notes is missing.

questions.question: Where did the man see the notebook?
choices: On a table near the window
At the lost and found desk
questions.explanation: The man says he saw it on the table near the window.

questions.question: Why is the notebook important to the woman?
choices: It contains notes for a quiz
questions.explanation: The woman says it has all her chemistry notes and she has a quiz tomorrow.

questions.question: What does the man suggest first?
choices: Checking the lost and found desk
questions.explanation: The man asks whether she checked the lost and found desk.

questions.question: What does the man offer to do if she cannot find it?
questions.explanation: He says he can lend her his notes from last week if she cannot find the notebook.

items.title: Academic talk about volcanic soil
items.ttsText: Narrator. Listen to part of a lecture in an earth science class. Professor. Although volcanic eruptions can be destructive, volcanic activity can also create very fertile soil over time. When volcanic rock breaks down, it releases minerals such as potassium, phosphorus, and iron. These minerals can support plant growth and make the soil valuable for agriculture. This process does not happen immediately after an eruption. Fresh volcanic material may be too hot, too rocky, or too unstable for plants. However, weathering gradually breaks the material into smaller particles, and organic matter begins to accumulate. In some regions, farmers have used volcanic soils to grow crops such as grapes, coffee, and vegetables. The fertility of volcanic soil helps explain why people sometimes continue to live near volcanoes despite the risks. Narrator. Now answer the questions.

questions.question: What is the lecture mainly about?
choices: How volcanic activity can create fertile soil
Why plants cannot grow near mountains
questions.explanation: The professor explains how volcanic rock can break down and create fertile soil.

questions.question: Which minerals are mentioned in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The professor mentions potassium, phosphorus, and iron.

questions.question: Why is fresh volcanic material not immediately good for plants?
choices: It may be too hot, rocky, or unstable
It has already become organic matter
It prevents all minerals from forming
questions.explanation: The professor says fresh volcanic material may be too hot, too rocky, or too unstable.

questions.question: What process breaks volcanic material into smaller particles?
questions.explanation: The professor says weathering gradually breaks the material into smaller particles.

questions.question: What does the professor imply about people living near volcanoes?
choices: They may stay because the soil can be useful for farming
They live there only because of tourism
questions.explanation: The fertility of volcanic soil helps explain why people sometimes live near volcanoes despite the risks.

items.title: Academic talk about animal communication
items.ttsText: Narrator. Listen to part of a lecture in a biology class. Professor. Animals communicate in many different ways, and their signals can serve several purposes. Some signals help animals attract mates, while others warn members of a group about danger. Birds, for example, may use songs to defend territory or attract partners. Certain mammals use scent markings to show that an area is occupied. Insects may communicate through movement. Honeybees perform a type of dance that can indicate the direction and distance of a food source. Communication signals are not always sounds; they may also involve color, posture, chemicals, or motion. Studying these signals helps scientists understand how animals survive, reproduce, and interact with their environments. Narrator. Now answer the questions.

questions.question: What is the lecture mainly about?
questions.explanation: The professor discusses various types of animal communication signals.

questions.question: According to the professor, why may birds use songs?
choices: To defend territory or attract partners
questions.explanation: The professor says birds may use songs to defend territory or attract partners.

questions.question: What do some mammals use scent markings for?
choices: To show that an area is occupied
To change the color of their fur
To hide from their own group
questions.explanation: The professor says certain mammals use scent markings to show that an area is occupied.

questions.question: What information can a honeybee dance communicate?
choices: The direction and distance of a food source
The size of a bird's nest
questions.explanation: The professor explains that honeybee dances can indicate direction and distance to food.

questions.question: What does the professor emphasize about communication signals?
choices: They can involve more than sound
They are used only by birds
questions.explanation: The professor says signals may involve color, posture, chemicals, or motion, not only sounds.

items.title: Academic talk about the history of maps
items.ttsText: Narrator. Listen to part of a lecture in a geography class. Professor. Maps have been used for thousands of years to represent space, movement, and relationships between places. Early maps were often created for practical purposes, such as marking trade routes, showing property boundaries, or helping travelers find water sources. Over time, mapmaking became more accurate as explorers, sailors, and scientists developed better methods for measuring distance and direction. The invention of printing allowed maps to be copied and distributed more widely. Later, aerial photography and satellite technology changed mapmaking again by providing detailed views of Earth's surface. Today, digital maps can be updated quickly and combined with information about traffic, weather, population, and land use. Although map technology has changed greatly, maps continue to help people understand where things are and how places are connected. Narrator. Now answer the questions.

questions.question: What is the lecture mainly about?
choices: The development and continuing importance of maps
How weather is predicted without technology
questions.explanation: The professor describes how maps have developed and why they remain useful.

questions.question: What was one practical purpose of early maps?
questions.explanation: The professor says early maps were used for marking trade routes, property boundaries, or water sources.

questions.question: What allowed maps to be copied and distributed more widely?
questions.explanation: The professor says printing allowed maps to be copied and distributed more widely.

questions.question: How did satellite technology affect mapmaking?
choices: It provided detailed views of Earth's surface
It prevented people from measuring distance
It was used only for ancient property boundaries
questions.explanation: The professor says aerial photography and satellite technology provided detailed views of Earth's surface.

questions.question: What does the professor imply about modern digital maps?
choices: They can combine many kinds of information
They are less useful than all early maps
questions.explanation: The professor says digital maps can combine information about traffic, weather, population, and land use.
